message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 2/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
National Life Group (NLG) / Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW) TERM LIFE INSURANCE products — comprehensive product reference. Cover: (1) the full current term lineup and product names (e.g. the "G" guaranteed level-premium series Term 10-G / 15-G / 20-G / 30-G, form series ICC18-20522, and any other current term products like LSW Level Term / annually-renewable term); (2) issue ages and available level term lengths; (3) the level-premium period then annually-renewable-to-age-95 structure, whether the policy has cash value/dividends (it does not); (4) the conversion privilege — window (first 20 years or to age 70 if earlier), no evidence of insurability, equivalent rate class, what permanent products it converts to; (5) the Accelerated Benefit Rider (ABR) living-benefit suite available on term (Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's) — cost (no additional premium), triggers, discounted/condition-specific payout, and per-insured lifetime benefit limits and how they vary by state; (6) premium structure and rate classes (Elite/Preferred/Select/Standard/Express, tobacco/non-tobacco); (7) state availability variations; (8) foreign-national / non-US-resident rules for term (minimum face, US nexus/solicitation/delivery requirements, whether Brazil/"B" country is eligible, whether term is even offered to foreign nationals). Prioritize NLG/LSW primary sources: nationallife.com, the Life Product Quick Reference Chart, agent guides, product guides, rider brochures, and the Foreign National Guidelines. Distinguish guaranteed/structural facts from rate-sheet-driven current rates (which change often). Produce a cited report with source URLs.

## Claim under review
"LSW's guaranteed level-premium term series in this guide comprises 10-G, 15-G/NG, 20-G/NG, and 30-G/NG, with maximum issue ages that shorten as the term lengthens: 10-G issues to age 75, 15-G/NG to age 75, 20-G/NG to age 70, and 30-G/NG to age 55 (all from issue age 18)."

**Source:** https://www.totalfinancial.com/carriers/downloads/lsw/lsw-uw1.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Ages 18-75: 10-G: All Face Amounts ... Ages 18-75: 15-G/NG: All Face Amounts ... Ages 18-70: 20-G/NG: All Face Amounts ... Ages 18-55: 30G/NG: All Face Amounts"

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim about LSW's guaranteed level-premium term series issue ages. Let me start by examining the primary source.
I'll fetch the primary source PDF to verify the specific issue age claims.

input.prompt: Extract all information about term life insurance products in the "G" series (10-G, 15-G, 15-NG, 20-G, 20-NG, 30-G, 30-NG). What are the exact issue age ranges for each product? Quote verbatim any tables or text listing "Ages 18-75", "Ages 18-70", "Ages 18-55" or similar, and which products those ages apply to. Also note the document date/title.

content.content:
I appreciate your request, but I need to be direct: the content provided is a PDF file in binary format with compression and encoding that makes it unreadable in plain text. The material shown contains PDF object streams, font definitions, and encoded content rather than human-readable text about insurance products.

To extract the specific information you're seeking about the "G" series term life insurance products (10-G, 15-G, 15-NG, 20-G, 20-NG, 30-G, 30-NG) and their exact issue age ranges, I would need:

1. **A readable text or properly decoded version** of the document, or
2. **The document title and date** so I can reference it accurately, or
3. **Direct access to the insurance product specifications** you're asking about

Without legible source material, I cannot reliably extract the age ranges ("Ages 18-75," "Ages 18-70," etc.) or provide verbatim quotations as you've requested—doing so would risk providing inaccurate information about insurance eligibility criteria, which could be consequential.

If you can share a text-readable version of this document or clarify which insurance provider's materials these are, I'd be happy to help extract the specific product details you need.

content.thinking:
Looking at the source material, the claim about issue ages and maximum ages for each guarantee period matches exactly what's stated—10-G and 15-G/NG go to age 75, 20-G/NG to age 70, and 30-G/NG to age 55, all starting from issue age 18.

But I need to be careful about the context here. This table appears in the Underwriting Requirements section with "Verified Std/Std Tobacco" listed in the left column, which suggests these might be the issue ages specific to that underwriting class rather than the overall maximum issue ages for the products themselves. The table structure shows different medical requirement categories with their corresponding age ranges, so I should verify whether these ages apply universally to the G-series products or only within this particular underwriting classification.

Looking at the claim against the actual quote text, each product's maximum issue age matches exactly: 10-G to 75, 15-G/NG to 75, 20-G/NG to 70, and 30-G/NG to 55. The claim appears well-supported by the verbatim quotes from the table.

Now I'm considering potential weaknesses in the source itself — totalfinancial.com is a third-party BGA/IMO redistribution of National Life's underwriting requirements document, not a primary source directly from the carrier.

The bigger concern is whether this document is current. It references 2013 LSW Term products, while the G-series (10-G through 30-G) represents the newer guaranteed level term line introduced around 2018. Without a clear document date, I can't be certain these issue ages still apply to current NLG products, though structural limits like maximum issue ages tend to be fairly stable features that don't change frequently. I should search for confirmation on the G-series issue age limits to verify whether these are truly the product maximums or just underwriting class boundaries.

content.text: The source text confirms the quote verbatim. Now let me check the document date and search for corroborating/contradicting evidence on the current G-series issue ages.

content.thinking:
I'm noticing a date inconsistency here. The document says it was last updated in November 2015, but the G-series products mentioned in it weren't introduced until 2018. That's a three-year gap that doesn't make sense. I need to look more carefully at whether "Last Updated November 2015" applies to the whole document or just a specific section, and whether there might have been later revisions I'm missing. rts the issue ages exactly as stated. The real question is whether this guide is current — it's dated November 2015 but references G-series products from 2018, which is odd. I should search for the current NLG G-series issue ages to see if they've changed since then. That said, the claim is carefully worded to describe what "this guide" says, and the quote supports that completely, so even if the guide is somewhat outdated, the claim accurately represents what it contains. Now I need to clarify the table structure — when it shows "Ages 18-75: 10-G," does that mean the product's issue age range is 18-75, or that the Verified Standard underwriting applies at those ages? doesn't change the accuracy of the claim—10-G does issue to age 75 as stated, even though that's the maximum across all rate classes rather than a universal cap. The claim is factually correct about the product's maximum issue age.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about National Life LSW term insurance products with 10-G, 15-G, 20-G, and 30-G guaranteed level terms:

## Product Overview

LSW Level Term 10-G offers premiums that are guaranteed and level for 10 years, LSW Level Term 15-G offers premiums that are guaranteed and level for 15 years, LSW Level Term 20-G offers premiums that are guaranteed and level for 20 years, and LSW Level Term 30-G offers premiums that are guaranteed and level for 30 years.

## Key Features

Level Term 15-G has guaranteed level premiums for 15 years, with coverage extending to age 95. The LSW Level Term 20-G is a term life insurance policy that is annually renewable to age 95, with premiums level for the first 20 years and increasing annually thereafter to attained age 95.

## Premium Structure

Guaranteed level premiums are based on issue age, sex, rate class and face amount.

## General Policy Characteristics

LSW Level Term 10, LSW Level Term 15, LSW Level Term 20, and LSW Level Term 30 (both G and NG plans) are low-cost, convertible term life insurance policies with level premium periods and level death benefits.

The search results don't provide specific issue age ranges for each product variant, but they confirm these are guaranteed level term products designed to provide competitive, affordable term life insurance coverage with customizable options.

content.thinking: I'm looking at the LSW Level Term Product Guide which breaks down issue ages by product type—10-G covers 18-75, while the 15-G through 30-G products have progressively narrower age ranges, with tobacco applicants facing even tighter restrictions. This corroborates the claim's numbers. Everything checks out across both sources—the maximum issue ages match the non-tobacco band, which is the correct interpretation of "maximum," and the product guide confirms all the specific ages and series compositions. The claim is well-supported and current, so it stands as accurate.

content.text:
The claim is fully verified. Let me record my assessment.

The claim states the G-series comprises 10-G, 15-G/NG, 20-G/NG, 30-G/NG with max issue ages 75/75/70/55, all from age 18.

**Primary source (verbatim match):** The totalfinancial.com underwriting guide (out.txt lines 621-624) reads exactly: "Ages 18-75: 10-G: All Face Amounts / Ages 18-75: 15-G/NG: All Face Amounts / Ages 18-70: 20-G/NG: All Face Amounts / Ages 18-55: 30G/NG: All Face Amounts." Every number in the claim matches verbatim.

**Independent corroboration:** A dedicated LSW Level Term Product Guide (theinsgroup.net) independently confirms each figure: 10-G "18 - 75"; 15-G/15-NG "18 - 75 Non-tobacco; except 18 - 70 for [tobacco]"; 20-G/20-NG "18 - 70 Non-tobacco; except 18 - 65 for [tobacco]"; 30-G/30-NG "18 - 55 Non-tobacco; except 18 - 50 for [tobacco]." The claim's stated maximums (75/75/70/55) correctly equal the widest (non-tobacco) band = the product maximum.

**Refutation attempts failed:** (1) Quote support — exact. (2) Contradicting evidence — none; a second source corroborates. (3) Source quality — two reproductions of official NLG/LSW product/underwriting guides, adequate for a structural product-spec claim. (4) Outdated — the primary guide's cover says "Last Updated November 2015," a minor concern, but the independent product guide corroborates the identical ages, and these are structural (not rate-driven) facts. (5) Not marketing fluff.

One minor nuance (not a refutation): the claim presents the numbers as flat maximums, whereas tobacco classes cap 5 years lower for 15/20/30. But the claim says "maximum issue ages," which correctly equals the non-tobacco band.

input.evidence: Claim VERIFIED, not refuted. Primary source (totalfinancial.com lsw-uw1.pdf) contains the supporting quote VERBATIM: "Ages 18-75: 10-G: All Face Amounts / Ages 18-75: 15-G/NG / Ages 18-70: 20-G/NG / Ages 18-55: 30G/NG" — matching all four issue-age maximums (75/75/70/55) and the age-18 floor exactly. Independently CORROBORATED by a dedicated LSW Level Term Product Guide (theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Level_Term_Product_Guide.pdf), which lists: 10-G "18-75"; 15-G/15-NG "18-75 Non-tobacco (18-70 tobacco)"; 20-G/20-NG "18-70 Non-tobacco (18-65 tobacco)"; 30-G/30-NG "18-55 Non-tobacco (18-50 tobacco)". The claim's stated maximums equal the widest (non-tobacco) band = the product maximum, so the numbers are correct. Series composition (10-G, 15-G/NG, 20-G/NG, 30-G/NG) confirmed in both sources. Minor caveats that do NOT refute: (a) primary guide cover reads "Last Updated November 2015," but the second product guide corroborates identical ages and these are structural (not rate-sheet) facts; (b) tobacco classes cap 5 years lower for the 15/20/30 terms, but the claim correctly says "maximum issue ages," which is the non-tobacco band. Source quality (two official NLG/LSW guide reproductions) matches the strength of a product-spec claim.
